headline: Glass Shines in Defeat as USC Survives Offensive Shootout
body: Arnold Glass put on a masterclass in the passing game, completing 30 of 46 attempts for 388 yards and three touchdowns, yet it proved insufficient as USC edged Oklahoma 35-32 in a game that hinged on precision and execution in the final moments. Glass's performance—efficient and creative—deserved better fortune, but USC's balanced attack and relentless receiver Burnett proved the difference when points mattered most.

The turning point arrived in the second half when USC's defense, anchored by Johnson's two sacks, began collapsing Oklahoma's pocket at critical junctures. While Glass exploited soft coverage downfield and McNeil hauled in two touchdown receptions for 89 yards, Oklahoma's ground game stalled at 78 yards, forcing Glass into increasingly difficult third-and-long situations. USC quarterback Moss took advantage of those breathing room moments, completing 25 of 48 passes for 439 yards while leaning heavily on Burnett's eight receptions for 201 yards—a performance that stretched Oklahoma's secondary thin.

Franklin's one-yard touchdown plunge and McNeil's two scoring grabs kept Oklahoma within striking distance throughout, but USC's methodical drive late in the game left no time for another Oklahoma response. The fumble by Oklahoma went unreturned when it mattered, and that single turnover differential ultimately sealed the result. Glass walks away as the statistical standout who nearly willed his team to victory; instead, USC's depth at receiver and defensive pressure in key moments proved decisive.
turningPoint: Johnson's two second-half sacks compressed Oklahoma's pocket and forced Glass into more difficult passing lanes, allowing USC to control rhythm and run down the clock in the final quarter.

unsungHero.name: Marcus Burnett
unsungHero.why: His 201 receiving yards and consistent third-down conversions were the mechanical engine of USC's drive, yet Glass earned Player of the Game honors despite being on the losing side.
